text:
Res Hall RA Fall Semester Responsibilities Overview
Residence Halls and RIT Inn - Full RA Guide
Programming
● Four self-made RA Programs. Each program will fall under Live, Learn, Belong,
Succeed.
● Two Campus Connect Programs. RAs will be responsible for signing up for two
different campus connect events each semester and bringing their residents to the
event. (RIT Inn RAs will only support if the CC is located at the Inn)
● One RC led All-Hall program. RAs will be responsible for collaborating with their
staff on a large-scale program targeted towards their entire community. This will
be led by RCs.
● One “Fest” event. Each RA will take their residents to an event each semester in our
majo

by RCs.
● One “Fest” event. Each RA will take their residents to an event each semester in our
major RIT festivals (Tiger Fest, Fall Fest, Brick City Homecoming, Freeze Fest,
Spring Fest).
● One Center Wide Event (CWE) Volunteer shift.
Resident Interactions
● One welcome interaction.
● One roommate agreement check-in. RAs will have touch point conversation with
residents to fully understand the agreements made. Also, stress agreement
update conversations as needed.
● Two Guided Connections.
● Outreaches as assigned by RCs.
● RA Duty Rotation.
Resident Communication and Education
● Four Bulletin Boards/Newsletters.
● One set of Door Decorations (one per resident).
● One Health and Safety Ins

in Boards/Newsletters.
● One set of Door Decorations (one per resident).
● One Health and Safety Inspection (and follow-up if needed).
● Ongoing updates distributed to their communities through group chat, email,
flyers, etc.
Meeting requirements
● Weekly staff meetings with supervisor and RA staff.
● Weekly one-on-one meetings with your supervisor.
● Monthly in-service training with the entire Center for Residence Life.

Guide for Nominating Peers for Awards
This guide is intended for those writing nominations for their peers. By following these
suggestions and understanding the criteria for each award, your submissions will better align
with the framework used for evaluation.
Tips for Submitting RAotM Nominations
● Highlight Specific Actions: Provide clear examples of how the RA met or exceeded
expectations. Use specific instances like successful events, handling conflicts, or
moments they went above and beyond to offer a concrete basis for consideration.
○ For example, instead of saying how someone is “always there for their residents”,
write about a specific time they supported their residents.
● Tie to C

s there for their residents”,
write about a specific time they supported their residents.
● Tie to Criteria: Align nomination details with the criteria areas.
○ For example, if nominating someone for the RA of the Month award, you could
specifically highlight their actions that align with the criteria “Community
Development”. To do this you could describe how the RA's visibility or event
facilitation created new connections that led to community development.
● Recognize Growth: If an RA has shown noticeable improvement over time, mention it.
Progress demonstrates commitment and a willingness to develop as a leader.
○ For example, you could write about how earlier in the semester the person y

o develop as a leader.
○ For example, you could write about how earlier in the semester the person you
are nominating struggled with incorporating a theme into their bulletin boards.
However, after working with their peers, their newest board seamlessly
incorporates their theme.
● Inclusivity and Cultural Awareness: Mention specific actions the RA has taken to make
their community more inclusive or culturally aware. These details can help set a nominee
apart, especially if they are proactive in promoting diversity and equity.
○ For example, instead of saying how someone “makes all their residents feel
included”, write about a specific ongoing strategy they incorporate into their
interactions

nts feel
included”, write about a specific ongoing strategy they incorporate into their
interactions with their residents that leave a feeling of belonging.
Award Criteria and Descriptions
Below are each of the Residence Life Monthly Award categories. Each award lists the criteria
areas that are reviewed.
RA & Professional Staff of the Month Awards

● Community Development: Evaluates the RA's visibility, approachability, and
engagement with residents, as well as their ability to create events and connect residents
to campus resources.
● Conflict Management: Assesses the RA's judgment in addressing conflicts and crises,
their application of policies, and the quality of their incident reports.
● Administrative Tasks: Focuses on reliability, proactive communication, meeting
deadlines, and organizational skills.
● Leadership: Considers how the RA motivates and inspires the team, acts as a role model,
and contributes to overall team morale.
● Team Work: Consider how the RA supports the initiatives of their team, the events, and
the individua

Team Work: Consider how the RA supports the initiatives of their team, the events, and
the individual needs of their peers.
● Inclusivity: Evaluates how the RA fosters a welcoming environment, demonstrates
cultural awareness, and supports diversity and equity efforts.
Golden Stapler (Educational Display)
● Content Presentation: The content is thoughtfully designed, informative, and engaging
or interactive for students.
● Educational Display Theme: Assesses how well the theme is integrated into the content
and its creativity and relevance.
● Time/Effort (Construction & Inclusivity): Evaluates the legibility of text, the layout’s
organization, and how the design reflects inclusivity and though

he legibility of text, the layout’s
organization, and how the design reflects inclusivity and thoughtful perspective.
Golden Mouse (Newsletter)
● Content Presentation: The content is informative, inspiring, and includes interactive
elements like puzzles or scavenger hunts to foster community.
● Newsletter Theme: Considers the execution and creativity of the theme and its
relevance to the residents' experiences.
● Time/Effort (Construction, Inclusivity, & Resources): Evaluates visual appeal,
organizational flow, inclusivity of perspectives, and the relevance and detail of the
resources provided to residents.

General Resources
Ombuds:
The RIT Ombuds office provides a safe and confidential place for students, faculty, and staff to
bring any kind of problem, dispute, or question.
Ombuds is a confidential resource.
Housing:
RIT Housing provides the best living environment for students pursuing successful college
experiences, where they can develop friendships, be inspired to work on creative projects, and
engage in new experiences in a safe, residential atmosphere.
The Conduct Office:
The Center for Student Conduct and Conflict Resolution designs and delivers a fair,
educationally based behavioral conduct process, as well as policy education and training, to the
RIT community.
The Academic Success C

duct process, as well as policy education and training, to the
RIT community.
The Academic Success Center:
At the Academic Success Center, it is our mission to assist you in developing the skills and
confidence you need to achieve both academic and personal success.
Student Wellness Resources
Counseling and Psychological Services (CaPS):
CaPS provides confidential and personalized services to meet the mental health needs of RIT
students.
CaPS is a confidential resource.
The Student Health Center:
Provides high-quality primary health care and education to students in a caring and supportive
environment.
Health Promotions: offers a variety of educational and engaging programs for students
thro

ironment.
Health Promotions: offers a variety of educational and engaging programs for students
throughout the academic year.
Student Engagement Resources
Fraternity and Sorority Life:

RIT is home to 32 diverse inter/national social fraternities and sororities focused on academics,
leadership, service and improving the overall collegiate experience for all students.
College Activities Board:
CAB provides a diverse and unique assortment of events to entertain and engage students
outside of the classroom.
Women, Gender, and Sexuality Resource Center:
Provides educational programs focusing on healthy relationships, gender-based issues, and
leadership.
Spirituality and Religious Life:
Cultivating engaged and vibrant religious, secular, and spiritual communities on campus that
explore deep questions of purpose, meaning, and value.
Spirituality and Religious Life is a confidenti

plore deep questions of purpose, meaning, and value.
Spirituality and Religious Life is a confidential resource.
Student Advocacy Resources
International Student Services:
We advise students, faculty, clubs, and departments on immigration. We also provide guidance
for cultural, academic, and personal matters.
The Disability Service Office:
The Disability Services Office is dedicated to facilitating equitable access to the full RIT
experience for students with disabilities. We value disability as diversity and work in
collaboration with campus partners to foster a welcoming, diverse, and inclusive campus
community.
Multicultural Center for Academic Success:
Building a community centered on tr

ive campus
community.
Multicultural Center for Academic Success:
Building a community centered on trust, the Multicultural Center for Academic Success (MCAS)
will strengthen the academic success of RIT’s AALANA (African, Latin, and Native American)
student population.
The Student Life Team:
SLT challenges and educates all deaf and hard-of-hearing students, providing resources and
support for their successful transition throughout college and after graduation.

RAs will be required to pick up the correct duty phone (based on their duty line
assignment) from the Gleason Service Desk (by 5:00 pm). Upon picking up the
phone, RAs will text the RC on duty to check in and notify them that they have picked
up the duty phone.
There will be a total of 6 RAs on Duty in the dorms, divided into three duty lines:
RA 1 and RA 2: Sol, DSP, Fish
RA 3 and RA 4: Ellingson, Peterson, Res Hall D, Gibson
RA 5 and RA 6: ABC, Gleason, Baker, Colby
Sundays – Thursdays, two sets of rounds must occur:

Round 1: between 7-8 pm

, Gleason, Baker, Colby
Sundays – Thursdays, two sets of rounds must occur:

Round 1: between 7-8 pm

Round 2: between 10-11 pm
RAs are on call from 5 pm to 8 am the following morning. The RA on duty should return
the duty phone to the Gleason Service Desk the following morning.
Fridays & Saturdays, three sets of rounds must occur:

Round 1: between 7-8 pm

Round 2: between 10-11 pm

Round 3: between 12-1 am
On Fridays and Saturdays, RAs are on call for 24 hours from 5 pm to 5 pm. At 5 pm on
the following day, RAs must arrange to hand off the phone to the next RA on duty since
the service desk is closed on Saturdays and Sundays. This communication should
occur on Slack.

Duty Swaps
Definitions
● Swap: when you swap/switch your entire duty assignment with another RA.
● Hold: When another RA holds the duty phone for you because of a temporary scheduling
conflict. Holds should only be used as a last resort and should not be longer than an hour.
If a hold lasts longer than an hour, it should be turned into a swap.
How to do a swap or hold
Step 1:
● Tag Alison Thompson (amtrla@rit.edu) on the Center Duty Calendar.
● Tag the RA who you are holding or swapping with. Include all relevant details about the
swap/hold.
○ Allison and the RA you tag will receive an email notifying them of your comment.
Step 2:
● The other RA who you tagged must approve the hold/swap for

otifying them of your comment.
Step 2:
● The other RA who you tagged must approve the hold/swap for the process to move
forward.
● This means that they should log into Google Drive, open the Center Duty Calendar, find
the comment you added, and write “Approved” in the comment box.
Step 3:
● Once the other RA approves the swap/hold, Alison will change the names on the duty
calendar to reflect the correct person for a given night.
● Alison will “resolve” the comment box once the change has been made.
No hold or switch is final until it has been resolved. Make sure that you check the
calendar to ensure that it reflects the changes correctly!
Note:
Emergency duty holds and switches that are last

ensure that it reflects the changes correctly!
Note:
Emergency duty holds and switches that are last minute need to go to the RC on Duty.
Alison does NOT respond to emails after business hours, on holidays, or over the weekend.
You should be tagging the RC on Duty directly (or your current RC) to ensure shifts are covered
appropriately if there is a last minute request!

Duty Swaps

id: Health and Safety Inspections - RA Instructions.docx.pdf::0
source: Health and Safety Inspections - RA Instructions.docx.pdf
text:
INSTRUCTIONS:
- RAs will conduct inspections from the doorway of each room. RAs
will look for obvious violations, and ask the residents to confirm
compliance with various items.
-If possible, all occupants of the room should be present for these
inspections
- All inspections should be tracked through this sheet. If you need to
file an IR, use this IR form
- Include a photo of the violation in the IR form
- It is likely that you will conduct a follow-up inspection on any
violations discovered. Your supervisor will instruct you on when these
should occur
Please contact your supervisor with any questions.

IR Tips and Tricks
1. Responding to an incident:
a. Be sure to introduce yourself to the residents so they know who you are.
b. Be direct with why you are knocking on the door (ie. “While conducting
rounds, I noticed loud music coming from this room and could hear it down
the hall. Were you aware of the volume?”)
c. Take time to look at your surroundings while talking to the resident.
d. Feel free to take notes while you are there to help you write the IR.
e. Work with your fellow RA on duty for support during the incident and in
writing the IR.
2. IR Writing:
a. Write in the third person
b. Be as specific and detailed as possible (for example - how much alcohol
was in the room and what kind

e as specific and detailed as possible (for example - how much alcohol
was in the room and what kind(s); how many posters were hanging from
the ceiling; what did you observe about the people in the room using direct
quotes when possible)
c. Always be objective! This document may be used outside of residence life;
please be professional and do not make assumptions or insert your
opinion about the situation.
d. Do not recommend what should happen next. You are documenting what
you see and observe in the moment so that a professional staff member
may make the “what next?” decision. If you have concerns or questions,
don’t hesitate to ask your supervisor or the RC on Duty.

e. Take time to write the IR well, using proper grammar (Grammarly is a
great tool if you struggle with this).
f. WRITE THE NARRATIVE/CONTENT IN A SEPARATE DOCUMENT! If
you type right into the IR link and it refreshes, you will lose all of your hard
work!

Navigating and Updating Outreach
Information in SBCT

Created by Creation Date Last Updated

SBCT Outreach process RA 9 Steps

STEP 1
After Clicking on the SBCT Outreach Click on Edit

STEP 2
Click on the Completed Check Box if the task is completed (if task is not
completed, do not check completed)

STEP 3
Type you summary of the SBCT outreach in the comments section below.
We need to know the Who, What, Where, and When of the interaction.

STEP 4
Click on Assigned To:

STEP 5
Type "sdsrla" or Scott Sheehan to reassign it to Associate Director Scott

ck on Assigned To:

STEP 5
Type "sdsrla" or Scott Sheehan to reassign it to Associate Director Scott

STEP 6
Click on Search after typing in Scott's name or user name.

STEP 7
Click on sdsrla or Scott Sheehan

STEP 8
Click on Save Changes after you have either completed the outreach, or
updated the comments section.

STEP 9
Click on CLOSE and the next time you refresh your dashboard the SBCT
outreach will fall off your list.

Outreaches Tips and Tricks
1. Know the context of the outreach. Is the student struggling with a mental health
concern? Were they transported for a medical injury? Are they missing classes?
Knowing the general context of the outreach can help you prepare to navigate
the conversation.
2. Use whatever approach makes sense to you - if you prefer a direct approach,
you can get right to the point and express the concern or reason for your follow
up. If you prefer to have lighter conversation and work into the topic of concern,
that works too! It is all about what works best for you.
3. If a student discloses anything to you regarding the following: violations of
University Policy, sexual/dating v

discloses anything to you regarding the following: violations of
University Policy, sexual/dating violence, sexual assault, harassment,
discrimination, self-harm behavior, and/or suicidal ideation, then you MUST
submit an IR.
a. If you are unsure if you should write an IR, ask your supervisor or the RC
on duty.
4. Feel free to take notes during the outreach to help you write your outreach
summary. You can always let the resident know that you are taking notes to
share with your supervisor to maintain transparency with them.
5. If a student wishes to speak to a confidential resource, refer them to the
following:
a. RIT CaPS
b. RIT Student Health Center
c. RIT Ombuds Office

d. RIT Spirituality and Religious Life
e. NTID CaPS

Progressive Discipline
Process

What is progressive discipline?
The Progressive Discipline Process is intended to address and correct behaviors
that hinder the successful completion of job responsibilities within the RA role.
There are 5 levels to the progressive discipline process. The level of severity and/or
definition of problematic performance are determined at the discretion of the
supervisor in consultation with the leadership team. Depending on the severity of the
misconduct, an RA may skip levels of the process.

leadership team. Depending on the severity of the
misconduct, an RA may skip levels of the process.

Level 1: Corrective Conversations
Informal, initial conversation
Not disciplinary in nature- an opportunity for timely
feedback
Should always be followed by an email summary

Examples of Corrective Conversation
Behaviors
First time late to a staff meeting
First time a response to an email or other correspondence
is delayed

st time late to a staff meeting
First time a response to an email or other correspondence
is delayed

Level 2: Initial Warning
First formal step of the process
Documented with a formal letter and uploaded into Starrez
May include an action plan but is not required to

Examples of Initial Warning Behaviors
Submitting a community development framework task late
Recurrence of lateness to a meeting
Any repeat behaviors in which a corrective conversation
has already been had

f lateness to a meeting
Any repeat behaviors in which a corrective conversation
has already been had

Level 3: Final Warning
Given when a staff member has not corrected behavior,
behavior increases in severity, or a more serious action
occurs
Includes a written warning and established action plan,
uploaded to Starrez

Examples of Final Warning Behaviors
A pattern of lateness to meetings
Not completing a community development task

inal Warning Behaviors
A pattern of lateness to meetings
Not completing a community development task

Level 4: Probation
The final corrective step- subsequent issues may result in
loss of position
Includes a written letter and established action plan which
are uploaded to Starrez

Examples of Probation Behaviors
Missing a duty shift
Unexcused absence from RA Training session or other
Center wide expectations

Level 5: Separation
Immediate Removal from the RA role
Decision to separate must be approved by the Director or
designee
Separation letter drafted and uploaded to Starrez

Examples of Separation Behaviors
Inappropriate Substance Use
Illegal Activity
Misuse of Access (Keys, Swipes, Physical Space)
Student Conduct Violations resulting in Probation or
Higher

What should an action plan look like?
Area for Improvement: Being on time with administrative tasks
Action steps:
1. Put reminders in phone for all deadlines for paperwork
2. Prior to leaving staff meetings, ensure all new dates and topics are added to calendar
3. Ask questions if deadlines are not clear
Success will look like:
1. All task

re added to calendar
3. Ask questions if deadlines are not clear
Success will look like:
1. All tasks related to the RA role will be completed by assigned deadlines
2. RA will have clear and open communication with supervisor on progress towards tasks, and
come to one on ones prepared with updates

QUESTIONS?

The Mission of the Center for Residence Life
The Center for Residence Life supports and provides safe and inclusive learning environments that foster engagement, a sense
of belonging and connection to the RIT community.
Mandatory Meeting/Training Dates*
● Employment Period: August 11, 2025 through May 10, 2026
● RA Training/Semester Opening:
a) All RA staff can start moving in on August 8th, 2025**
b) All RA Staff start training August 11th, 2025
● Inservice Training
a) Two times a semester on Monday, between 7pm - 10pm
▪ Specific dates and time to be shared during Fall Training
● Winter Closing:
a) December 18th, 2025 at 2 p.m.
● Winter RA Training: (subject to change if the academic calend

ng:
a) December 18th, 2025 at 2 p.m.
● Winter RA Training: (subject to change if the academic calendar is modified):
a) New RA Staff (January 2026 hires): January 7th, 2026
b) Returning RA Staff: January 9th, 2026
● End of RA Commitment (subject to change if the academic calendar is modified):
a) May 10th, 2026 at 2 p.m.
*Any date changes made at the discretion of the Center for Residence Life will be communicated without delay to Resident
Advisors. Adjustments can be made with advance notice for academic and co-op commitments, any commitment or travel that
prevents arrival prior to 8/11/25 may result in ineligibility for the position.
**Due to summer construction, your room may not be avail

y result in ineligibility for the position.
**Due to summer construction, your room may not be available at the beginning of the August training. We will work with RAs
on a case-by-case basis to find a temporary room until your permanent room has been cleared for student move-in.
Minimum Employment Qualifications:
The following criteria must be met in order for a student to be in the RA position:
● Meet all Rochester Institute of Technology student employment eligibility requirements
● Enrolled as a full-time student at RIT
○ Undergraduate students: minimum of 12 credits per semester*
○ Graduate students: minimum of 9 credits per semester*
■ *An exception may be made if an RA is in their las

te students: minimum of 9 credits per semester*
■ *An exception may be made if an RA is in their last semester until graduation, and the RA doesn’t
require full time credit hours. RAs in this position may email the Associate Director in their supervision
line to discuss
● Possess minimum GPAs
○ Undergraduate RAs: 2.4 cumulative and semester GPA
○ Graduate RAs: 3.0 cumulative and semester GPA
● Must be available for staff meetings and/or inservice meetings on Monday nights between 7pm-10pm
● Must be enrolled in the RIT emergency alert system: RIT Alert
● Cannot hold a status of Probation or higher through the RIT conduct process
● Must complete all required training sessions prior to the star

her through the RIT conduct process
● Must complete all required training sessions prior to the start of employment
Position Description Overview:
The Resident Advisor (RA) position is a part-time student leadership opportunity within the Center for Residence Life. The RA
lives and works with a community of residents in an assigned housing area at Rochester Institute of Technology. The RA serves
as a peer educator, resource, and role model to residents. RAs are supervised by Residence Coordinators (RC) or Apartment

Residence Coordinators (ARC) to create an engaged living-learning community and execute learning strategies to meet
departmental goals of student personal development and communi

d execute learning strategies to meet
departmental goals of student personal development and community building. Most RA responsibilities occur during evening
and weekend hours.
Resident Advisor (RA) Agreement
Responsibilities and Tasks

A - Administrative Tasks
● Participate in a Resident Advisor Duty rotation (Global Village and RIT Inn RAs excluded).
○ Residence Halls: While on Duty, RAs must be on the Residence Hall side of campus between 5:00pm-8:30am (at
all times while in possession of the duty phone). RAs must conduct rounds as well as provide support to
students and Residence Life staff while on duty. RAs must stay on dormside while on duty, and should not go
past the Greek Lawn.
○ Graduate RAs: While on Duty, RAs must be within a 10 minute radius of the RIT campus at all times while in
possession of the duty phone. If off campus when called to respond, disclose your location and provide the RC
on Call an estimate o

f off campus when called to respond, disclose your location and provide the RC
on Call an estimate of when you can expect to arrive on scene.
● Provide a secondary point of contact in addition to the RA’s RIT email address. Be currently enrolled in the RIT
emergency alert system.
● Attend all training sessions, staff development activities, health and safety inspections, open and closing procedures
(where applicable), and other designated activities.
● Attend all staff meetings and one-on-one meetings with your supervisor.
● Check your staff office mailbox (where applicable) and RIT email daily.
● Complete and submit all necessary paperwork in a thorough and timely manner (i.e., Guided Conne

y.
● Complete and submit all necessary paperwork in a thorough and timely manner (i.e., Guided Connections, event
planning paperwork, budget paperwork, performance evaluations, etc.).
● Create building/complex flyers, door decorations, and educational displays as designated by your supervisor.
● Must check the Center for Residence Life Slack daily.

B - Community Development
● Remain on campus during most weekends. You must obtain permission from your supervisor prior to leaving campus
for consecutive evenings or consecutive weekends.
● Residence Hall RAs: Keep students informed by facilitating floor meetings and posting flyers and advertisements placed
in your RA mailbox.
● Have frequent and meaningful conversations with your residents in order to get to know them.
● Assist resident transition to campus and encourage making the most of the RIT experience by emphasizing awareness
of various opportunities.
● Complete Community Experience and Campus Connect requirements as directed by the Center for Residence Life.
● Graduate and Global V

nd Campus Connect requirements as directed by the Center for Residence Life.
● Graduate and Global Village RAs: Keep students informed through the electronic newsletter and social media, as well as
informal one-on-one conversations and posting flyers and advertisements provided to you.

C - Resident Communication
● Be available and accessible to your residents by making a strong commitment to being a presence within your
community.
● Establish positive rapport with residents in your community in order to create and maintain a friendly, inclusive
atmosphere conducive to academic success and group living.
● Serve as a resource or referral for information concerning RIT life.
● Encourage dialogue between roommates/neighbors and manage foreseeable conflict.
● Complete Guided Connections with each student as directed by the Center for Residence Life.

D - Crisis Management and Policy Enforcement
● Encourage students to take responsibility for their actions through clear and appropriate explanation of all University
policies and procedures.
● Confront, document, and report all student violations of the Housing Terms and Conditions and Student Code of
Conduct immediately following the incident through the use of an Incident Report (IR).
2

● Confront, document, and report all emergency and facility incidents immediately following the incident through the use
of an IR, FMS/Housing Facilities Work Order, or call to Public Safety.
● Assist with fire alarms in your area (Residence Hall RAs only).
● Utilize master keys only for job-related dutie

fire alarms in your area (Residence Hall RAs only).
● Utilize master keys only for job-related duties. Return master keys to their appropriate locations immediately after use
(Residence Hall RAs only)
● For non-policy violations, submit appropriate documentation through MyLife

E - Evaluation and Progressive Discipline
● Your performance as a Resident Advisor (RA) will be formally evaluated each semester by your direct supervisor.
Performance evaluations, including scores and written feedback, will be key factors considered in decisions regarding
rehiring for subsequent semesters.
● If you fail to meet expectations in any aspect of your RA responsibilities, concerns will typically be addressed using the
Center for Residence Life’s progressive discipline process. The process involves corrective conversations, initial warning,
final warning, probation, and separation from the role.
● In cases involving serious or severe infractions, higher-level disciplinary measures

rom the role.
● In cases involving serious or severe infractions, higher-level disciplinary measures, including immediate probation or
separation, may be applied without prior warnings.
● If your performance consistently does not align with the expectations and standards set forth by the Center for
Residence Life, you may not be invited to return to the RA position in future semesters. Examples of performance
concerns that may lead to non-renewal include, but are not limited to:
○ Lack of responsiveness to feedback
○ Disengagement from the RA role
○ Unprofessional conduct
○ Unsatisfactory evaluations or inadequate improvement following previous feedback
● Should there be any possibility of n

uations or inadequate improvement following previous feedback
● Should there be any possibility of non-renewal, your supervisor will engage in ongoing conversations with you
regarding these performance concerns prior to a final decision being communicated.
● If you wish to appeal a disciplinary decision or non-renewal, appeals should be submitted via email to an Associate
Director of Residence Life. If you wish to further appeal the decision, an additional appeal can be submitted via email to
the Director of Residence Life.

F - Re-application
● If you wish to continue for the 2026-2027 academic year, you must participate in the RA Intent to Return process as
specified by the Center for Residence Life. For RAs on campus, this will occur in staff meetings, for RAs who are away
from campus (such as on co-op or study abroad) a notification will be sent via email

G - Grades
Maintain the following semester and cumulative GPAs
● Undergraduate RAs: Semester and cumulative grade point average above a 2.4
● Graduate RAs: Semester and cumulative grade point average of 3.0 or above
Grades will be reviewed at the conclusion of each academic semester while you are employed as an RA. Failure to meet the
Center’s GPA requirements will result in a review of your status with the Center and possible action through the Progressive
Discipline process. Each case will be reviewed individually and an appropriate response determined. A list of likely outcomes is
detailed below:
● Cumulative GPA dropping below a 2.4 will likely result in immediate termination from the RA

below:
● Cumulative GPA dropping below a 2.4 will likely result in immediate termination from the RA position
● Semesterly GPA between 2.00 and 2.39 with a cumulative above a 2.4, will likely result in probation through the
Center’s Progressive Discipline Process
● Semesterly GPA below a 2.00 will likely result in immediate termination from the RA position regardless of cumulative
GPA
● Failure to meet the GPA requirements for a second consecutive will result in termination for the RA position

RA Response Playbook — Quick Guide to Handling Incidents
(Clean text version of the tables that did not survive PDF extraction.)

=== Scenario response guide: when to involve Public Safety (PS), when to call the RC on Duty, and which IR to file ===

Emergency Situation (fire, flood, major issue): Evacuate if necessary and follow emergency procedures; report the incident immediately. Always involve Public Safety in emergencies. Notify the RC for awareness and coordination. File a Maxient IR for policy violations or if PS was called.

the RC for awareness and coordination. File a Maxient IR for policy violations or if PS was called.

Person Hurt or in Danger: Ensure safety; call Public Safety for medical or safety emergencies. Call PS for immediate assistance. Inform the RC about the situation and any follow-up needed. File a Maxient IR if PS was involved or for significant policy issues.

Interpersonal Issues: Listen and support; mediate if appropriate; document the situation. If it escalates or becomes threatening, call Public Safety. Contact the RC for guidance or if mediation is needed. File a MyLife IR for roommate conflicts or students of concern.

guidance or if mediation is needed. File a MyLife IR for roommate conflicts or students of concern.

Unhealthy Relationship: Offer support and resources; refer to counseling services; document concerns. Call PS if there is immediate danger. Consult the RC for advice and additional resources. File a MyLife IR for students of concern.

Title IX Issues (sexual violence or harassment): Ensure safety; document details. Call PS if there is an immediate threat or danger. Notify the RC to coordinate support. File a Maxient IR for policy violations or if PS was called.

r. Notify the RC to coordinate support. File a Maxient IR for policy violations or if PS was called.

Disengaged Student: Reach out with support and resources; encourage engagement. Involve PS if there is concern for the student's safety. Contact the RC if the disengagement impacts the residence hall. File a MyLife IR for students of concern.

Unhealthy Relationship with Food: Offer support; refer to counseling and health services; document any concerns. Call PS if the situation is life-threatening. Consult the RC for additional resources and follow-up. File a MyLife IR for students of concern.

ng. Consult the RC for additional resources and follow-up. File a MyLife IR for students of concern.

Potential Intoxication and Concerned Roommates: Assess the situation; ensure safety and provide support. Call PS if there is risk of harm or intoxication-related issues. Inform the RC to discuss follow-up actions. File a Maxient IR for policy violations (e.g., alcohol, cannabis).

Bias-Related Issues: Document the incident; offer support to those affected. Involve PS if the situation escalates or involves physical threats. Notify the RC to address the situation. File a Maxient IR for policy violations or if PS was involved.

tify the RC to address the situation. File a Maxient IR for policy violations or if PS was involved.

Suicide Ideation: Ensure immediate safety; refer to counseling and crisis services; document the situation. Call PS immediately for a welfare check. Inform the RC for support and coordination with mental health services. File a MyLife IR for non-urgent student concerns.

Sleeping Hours / Use of Room: Address complaints respectfully; enforce policies as needed. Involve PS if there is a disruption or safety concern. Contact the RC if policies are repeatedly violated. File a Maxient IR for policy violations (e.g., quiet hours).

RC if policies are repeatedly violated. File a Maxient IR for policy violations (e.g., quiet hours).

Marijuana Use: Document the situation; refer to campus policies. Call PS if there is a significant policy violation or safety risk. Inform the RC about repeated violations. File a Maxient IR for policy violations (e.g., cannabis use).

Pets: Review housing policies; document the situation; address unauthorized pets with sensitivity. Call PS if there is an immediate threat to safety or health. Notify the RC. File a Maxient IR for policy violations (e.g., unauthorized pets).

safety or health. Notify the RC. File a Maxient IR for policy violations (e.g., unauthorized pets).

Roommate Privacy Concerns: Address concerns with empathy and confidentiality; facilitate mediation if needed. Involve PS if there is a safety or privacy breach. Contact the RC for mediation or ongoing issues. File a MyLife IR for roommate conflicts or privacy concerns.

Quiet Hours: Remind residents of the quiet hours policy; address complaints diplomatically. Call PS if quiet-hours violations escalate or involve conflicts. Inform the RC of repeated issues. File a Maxient IR for repeated violations or conflicts.

conflicts. Inform the RC of repeated issues. File a Maxient IR for repeated violations or conflicts.

Self-Harm: Ensure safety; provide immediate support and refer to mental health services. Call PS if there is an immediate threat or risk. Inform the RC for follow-up and resources. File a MyLife IR for students of concern.

Concerns about Loneliness and Homesickness: Offer support and resources; encourage connection with campus services. Call PS if there is a significant risk. Consult the RC for ongoing support. File a MyLife IR for non-urgent student concerns.

=== Which IR form should you use? ===

ng support. File a MyLife IR for non-urgent student concerns.

=== Which IR form should you use? ===

Maxient IR: Use for policy violations (e.g., alcohol, harassment, cannabis, covered smoke detector), or if Public Safety was called or present during the incident.

Health and Safety IR: Use for violations found during Health and Safety checks and Closing that are NOT policy violations (e.g., tapestries, candles, e-conveyances).

MyLife IR: Use for students of concern (non-urgent), and for roommate or interpersonal conflicts.

=== Quiet Hours policy ===

ts of concern (non-urgent), and for roommate or interpersonal conflicts.

=== Quiet Hours policy ===

Sunday through Thursday: 11 p.m. to 7 a.m.
Friday and Saturday: 1 a.m. to 7 a.m.
24-hour courtesy hours are always in effect, and quiet hours apply during exam weeks.

=== Title IX response ===

tesy hours are always in effect, and quiet hours apply during exam weeks.

=== Title IX response ===

If a resident discloses a potential Title IX violation (or another serious incident) to an RA for the first time, the RA must provide this exact statement to the person making the disclosure: "You have the right to make a report to university police or campus security, local law enforcement, and/or state police or choose not to report the incident to your institution; to be protected by the institution from retaliation for reporting an incident; and to receive assistance and resources from your institution."

=== Campus resources (what they help with and where to find them) ===

Office of Student Conduct and Conflict Resolution: manages student conduct cases related to policy violations; guidance on the conduct process and conflict resolution. Student Alumni Union (SAU), Room 1150.
Student Health Center: medical services, primary care, health counseling, and mental health support; resources for health concerns including nutrition and wellness. August Health Center, Building 9.
Counseling and Psychological Services (CaPS): confidential psychological counseling and mental health services, including individual and group therapy, crisis intervention, and workshops. August Health Center, Building 9.
Spectrum Support Program: support and accommodations for neurodiverse st

Health Center, Building 9.
Spectrum Support Program: support and accommodations for neurodiverse students, including those on the autism spectrum; academic and social support. Student Development Center (SDC), Room 1120.
Q Center (LGBTQ+ Services): support and advocacy for LGBTQ+ students; resources, counseling, and educational programs. Student Alumni Union (SAU), Room A640.
Ombuds Office: confidential, impartial support for resolving conflicts and navigating university policies. Student Alumni Union (SAU), Room 1150.
Title IX Office: addresses sexual harassment, sexual assault, and relationship violence; confidential support and help with reporting. Student Alumni Union (SAU), Room 1150.

ship violence; confidential support and help with reporting. Student Alumni Union (SAU), Room 1150.
Public Safety: campus safety and security; emergency response, crime prevention. Grace Watson Hall.
Residence Life and Housing: on-campus housing and residence life programming; help with housing issues, roommate conflicts, and community building. Grace Watson Hall, Suite 1.
Case Management: personalized support and coordination for students with complex academic, personal, or health-related issues; connects students to campus and community resources. Student Development Center (SDC), Room 1120.
Foodshare: free non-perishable food and personal care items for students in need. FoodShare West (1

Foodshare: free non-perishable food and personal care items for students in need. FoodShare West (113 Riverknoll) and FoodShare East (Mark Ellingson Hall, Room 1050).
Dean of Students Office: support and advocacy for personal or academic challenges, crisis intervention, and resource referrals. Student Development Center (SDC), Suite 1120.
Tiger Center: manage academic schedule, register for classes, view grades, and access academic services.
RIT Career Connect: explore career opportunities, connect with employers, and manage the job search.
RIT Mobile: campus maps, shuttle schedules, dining options, events calendar (iOS and Android).
RIT Safe: emergency contacts, campus safety information,

ptions, events calendar (iOS and Android).
RIT Safe: emergency contacts, campus safety information, and reporting safety concerns to Public Safety (iOS and Android).
=== After-hours and hotline numbers ===

RIT Public Safety (24/7 emergency response and campus safety): 585-475-3333.
Counseling and Psychological Services (CaPS) Emergency (after-hours crisis and mental health support): 585-475-2261. After hours, call the RIT Public Safety number above for urgent psychological support.
RIT Hotline (anonymous support and reporting for harassment, misconduct, or safety concerns): 585-475-2222.
National Suicide Prevention Lifeline (24/7 crisis support): 988.
Crisis Text Line (24/7 text support): text "HELLO" to 741741.
New York State Domestic Violence and Sexual Assault Hotline (24/7): 1-800-942-6906.
RIT Student Health Center After Hours (urgent medical guidance when the center is closed): 585-475-2
RIT Student Health Center After Hours (urgent medical guidance when the center is closed): 585-475-2255.
=== Do This, Not That (RA conduct during an incident) ===

Do: be assertive and confident; use positive body language; articulate policy; articulate the necessity of your presence; announce yourself and your position before entering a room; ask to enter the room; do not touch a person or any of their items; ask for IDs of everyone in the room; remain calm and professional; inform students of the next steps in the process; tell students you have documented them; use positive communication, questioning, and active listening; problem-solve during the incident; call backup when needed and appropriate; keep control of the room; speak to residents separately when appropriate; do not take sides; refer students to appropriate resources; stay community-focus

y when appropriate; do not take sides; refer students to appropriate resources; stay community-focused; follow up after an incident; keep the privacy of incidents, conduct, and mental-health concerns.

Not: being aggressive or insufficiently assertive; poor body language; inability to articulate policy or your purpose; failing to announce who you are; keying into a room without RD/GHR approval; touching a person or their items; failing to ask for IDs; getting easily worked up; promising a conduct outcome; failing to tell students they have been documented; difficulty communicating; failing to call backup; losing control of the room; failing to separate residents when needed; taking sides; failing to refer students to resources; being punitive; failing to follow up; speaking about conduct cases where other students can hear.

=== General tips ===

ling to follow up; speaking about conduct cases where other students can hear.

=== General tips ===

Always prioritize safety and well-being. Document all incidents accurately in the appropriate system. Maintain confidentiality and respect privacy. Seek advice and support from supervisors when needed. Never hesitate to involve Public Safety or the RC for assistance.

Which IR form should you use?
● Maxient IR:
○ Policy violations (ie. alcohol, harassment, cannabis, smoke
detector covered, etc.)
○ If Public Safety was called (or was present during the incident)
● Health and Safety IR:
○ Violations found during Health and Safety checks and Closing
(that are not policy violations: ie. tapestries, candles,
e-conveyances)
● myLife IR:
○ Students of concern (non-urgent)
○ Roommate/interpersonal conflicts

SBCT Star Rez Process RA Outreach
Step 1: RC assigns a “Task” to the RA of the area that the student lives in. (An email notification
should be sent to the RA shortly after the task is assigned)
Step 2: The “Task” should note: Descriptions “SBCT Outreach- (copy and paste the
“Comments” narrative)”, Start Date is that time and date, Due Date is 48 hours from the Start
Date, Type is “Outreach SBCT”, Status is “Assigned”, Priority is “.TASK: Priority”, Assigned
To: (RA of residential area).
Step 3: RC moves the case to “Outreach Assigned to RA” Click “Assigned” and “This Step will
be assigned to” Scott Sheehan Click “Save”.
Step 4a: The RA will see the case on their dashboard, click into the ca

Scott Sheehan Click “Save”.
Step 4a: The RA will see the case on their dashboard, click into the case and after completing the
outreach the RA will click into “Task” on the left side of the screen, click View, click Edit
Information, Click Complete, Click under “Status” Complete, and under the comments section
write a summary of their interaction with the student, then click save changes.
Step 4b: If the RA is reaching out multiple times unsuccessfully they should under the comments
section write down each unsuccessful attempt and click save changes. They should not mark the
Task as “Complete” until they have spoken to the resident and completed an outreach summary.
Step 5: Once the Outreac

” until they have spoken to the resident and completed an outreach summary.
Step 5: Once the Outreach is marked as complete reassign the Task to Scott Sheehan by clicking
on “Assigned” and typing in Scott Sheehan. Scott will move the case along and it will fall off the
RA’s dashboard. Scott will then close the case.
